employee_groups_covered: Police and fire (JLMC); all other public employees including teachers, DPW, clerical/admin (Ch. 150E Section 9)
impasse_process_type: Two-track: JLMC-ordered compulsory arbitration for police/fire; mediation plus advisory fact-finding plus rare voluntary arbitration for everyone else
binding_interest_arbitration_available: yes for police/fire; technically yes but rare/voluntary-only for everyone else
primary_source_used: Chapter 589 Acts of 1987 (JLMC); M.G.L. c.150E Section 9; Mass.gov DLR and JLMC pages
notes: Already independently verified three times in this project (teachers DPW clerical/admin) prior to this audit
project_implication: Baseline case; confirms the project's own prior findings

impasse_process_type: Conventional binding arbitration with 9 statutory criteria and an economic cap
primary_source_used: N.J.S.A. 34:13A-14 to -21 (Police and Fire Public Interest Arbitration Reform Act 1977 amended by P.L.2010 c.105); NJ PERC official page
confidence: high for police/fire; non-safety comparison not verified
notes: Non-safety negative is inferred from statute's explicit title/scope, not directly confirmed

teachers_covered: not verified this session (background knowledge suggests no)
primary_source_used: Civil Service Law Section 209.4 (Taylor Law 1974); NY State Senate official law text; PERB FAQ page
secondary_source_used: Empire Center for Public Policy (flagged as fiscally-conservative advocacy source for framing claims only)
confidence: high for police/fire; non-safety negative unverified
notes: Recent amendment requires heavier ability-to-pay weighting for fiscally eligible municipalities

primary_source_used: Public Act 312 of 1969, MCL 423.231 et seq.; Michigan Legislature official text
confidence: high for police/fire; non-safety negative unverified
notes: Act 312 described as Michigan joining Rhode Island and Pennsylvania in this policy choice

teachers_covered: not confirmed this session -- possible separate chapter not ruled out
impasse_process_type: Binding arbitration for unresolved issues after bargaining
compulsory_or_voluntary: compulsory (statutory right to arbitration as alternative to denied strike right)
primary_source_used: R.I. Gen. Laws Ch.28-9.1 28-9.2 28-9.5; RI General Assembly official statute site
confidence: high for the three confirmed chapters
notes: Most granular state found -- separate chapters for municipal police state police and fire; open question whether a teacher-specific chapter also exists
project_implication: Confirms multi-state pattern; flags a follow-up question on teacher coverage

teachers_covered: not verified this session; background knowledge (unverified) suggests a limited strike right instead of arbitration for most non-safety public employees including teachers under a separate Public Employe Relations Act
impasse_process_type: Binding arbitration, narrow judicial review only (jurisdiction regularity nonexistent-power constitutional-violation questions only)
primary_source_used: Act 111 of 1968, 43 P.S. Sections 217.1-217.10; PA General Assembly official text; PA appellate case law
secondary_source_used: Pennsylvania Municipal League (flagged as municipal-advocacy source for framing claims only)
confidence: high for police/fire; teacher strike-right claim explicitly unverified
notes: If confirmed, PA's non-safety trade-off (strike right instead of arbitration) would be structurally opposite to Massachusetts's no-strike model
project_implication: High-value follow-up target given direct relevance to project's institutional-trade-off framing

police_fire_specific: yes for fire; no comparable police statute found this session
impasse_process_type: Bargaining plus arbitration for unresolved issues
compulsory_or_voluntary: compulsory (available on request when bargaining unresolved)
primary_source_used: W.S. Sections 27-10-101 to 27-10-109 (Title 27 Labor and Employment Ch.10 Collective Bargaining for Fire Fighters); Wyoming Statutes via Justia aggregation of official text
confidence: high for fire; police-specific equivalent not found (not confirmed absent)
notes: Corrects an earlier bounded search that only found Wyoming's unrelated general Uniform Arbitration Act (Title 1 Ch.36)
project_implication: Narrows the national scan's implied police-and-fire framing to fire-only for Wyoming specifically

impasse_process_type: Arbitration as the statutory alternative to a denied strike right
binding_interest_arbitration_available: yes (core mechanism confirmed; full binding/compulsory mechanics not read in depth)
primary_source_used: Title 11 O.S. Section 51-101 et seq. (Fire and Police Arbitration Law); Oklahoma Statutes via Justia aggregation
confidence: moderate-high for the core fact; full mechanics not verified in depth
notes: Reused from the original national-scan session's research; not re-searched this session

employee_groups_covered: All covered public employees including teachers, with narrower bargaining scope and different arbitration criteria for non-public-safety units since a 2017 reform
police_fire_specific: no -- general statute; safety-favoring asymmetry operates through bargaining scope and arbitration criteria rather than arbitration availability
dpw_clerical_admin_covered: presumed yes (general public-employee statute), not specifically verified for DPW/clerical-admin titles
impasse_process_type: Mediation then binding final-offer-selection arbitration if impasse persists 10 days, available on either party's request
binding_interest_arbitration_available: yes for essentially all covered public employees
compulsory_or_voluntary: compulsory in the sense that either party alone can trigger it once the statutory precondition is met
primary_source_used: Iowa Code Ch.20 Section 20.22 (Binding arbitration); 2017 House File 291 enrolled text; Iowa Legislature official code archive
notes: The clearest documented exception in this audit to non-safety-lacks-compulsory-arbitration; but the 2017 reform introduced its own safety-favoring asymmetry via bargaining scope (mostly wages only for non-safety units of less than 30% public-safety composition) and arbitration criteria (removed ability-to-pay and past-contract consideration added mandatory public/private wage comparison) for non-safety units specifically
project_implication: Most consequential finding of this audit -- both confirms and adds nuance to the project's Iowa counterexample claim

employee_groups_covered: All state and local public employees including teachers police and firefighters -- no exceptions
police_fire_specific: no -- prohibition applies uniformly including to police/fire
teachers_covered: covered by the prohibition (no bargaining rights)
dpw_clerical_admin_covered: covered by the prohibition (no bargaining rights)
impasse_process_type: None -- collective bargaining agreements are void by statute
primary_source_used: G.S. 95-98 (1959); companion strike prohibition G.S. 95-98.1; North Carolina General Assembly official statute page
notes: Enacted in 1959 following a Mecklenburg County police unionization attempt; one of the clearest most direct statutory findings in this audit
project_implication: Confirms that in at least one state no CBA-based mechanism map is possible for any occupation safety or non-safety

employee_groups_covered: Reported to be all public employees per secondary-source consensus; primary statutory mechanism not clearly identified this session
police_fire_specific: reported no (uniform prohibition) not independently confirmed via primary source this session
teachers_covered: reported covered by the prohibition per secondary sources
dpw_clerical_admin_covered: reported covered by the prohibition per secondary sources
primary_source_used: S.C. Code Title 8 Ch.11 payroll-deduction restriction for bargaining-related associations (partial primary lead); 1972 SC Attorney General opinion citing a superseded 1962 code section
secondary_source_used: Ballotpedia public-sector union policy page; CEPR policy report on state public-sector bargaining regulation; New Mexico PELRB multi-state legal compilation
confidence: moderate -- practical prohibition well-supported by secondary consensus; primary statutory citation needs follow-up
notes: Flagged explicitly as the weakest-sourced entry in this audit; South Carolina's legal architecture (discouraging bargaining via payroll-deduction restriction) may differ structurally from North Carolina's direct void-and-illegal statute
project_implication: Do not cite South Carolina with the same confidence as North Carolina until a cleaner primary citation is found

employee_groups_covered: Local public employees at each locality's option since 2021 (including public school employees, excluding constitutional officers); state employees not covered by this local-option statute
teachers_covered: yes if a locality adopts an ordinance (explicitly includes public school employees)
dpw_clerical_admin_covered: yes if a locality adopts an ordinance
impasse_process_type: Local-option bargaining authorization; no impasse/arbitration mechanism identified in the statute itself
binding_interest_arbitration_available: no arbitration mechanism found in the statute; bargaining itself is opt-in with no compulsory impasse backstop
compulsory_or_voluntary: not applicable (bargaining is locally optional; no arbitration compulsion found)
primary_source_used: Va. Code Section 40.1-57.2 (effective May 1 2021); Code of Virginia official website law.lis.virginia.gov
secondary_source_used: general secondary legal commentary describing the pre-2020 prohibition as resting on a 1977 Virginia Supreme Court decision (not independently re-verified this session)
confidence: high for the current law; pre-2020 legal basis not re-verified
notes: Strikes remain prohibited regardless of whether a locality adopts a bargaining ordinance
project_implication: Directly relevant to the DPW/clerical-admin generalizability caveat that bargaining rights are not universal and can change by state reform

employee_groups_covered: General public employees (severely restricted bargaining scope since 2011); police fire and state troopers/inspectors (exempted from most 2011 restrictions)
police_fire_specific: partially -- 2011 Act 10 exempted public safety employees from restrictions applied to everyone else rather than creating a safety-specific arbitration mechanism from scratch
teachers_covered: yes but with bargaining scope restricted to wages only capped at inflation since 2011
dpw_clerical_admin_covered: yes with the same restricted scope as teachers since 2011
impasse_process_type: General-employee bargaining restricted to wage-only inflation-capped negotiation with no arbitration; public-safety employees retained broader pre-2011-style bargaining rights
binding_interest_arbitration_available: not clearly an arbitration mechanism for either group in sources reviewed this session -- the core feature is a bargaining-scope restriction not an arbitration backstop
compulsory_or_voluntary: not applicable in the arbitration sense; the compulsory/voluntary distinction here is about bargaining-scope restriction not arbitration
primary_source_used: none directly reviewed this session (primary Act 10 text and the December 2024 court opinion not pulled)
secondary_source_used: Wikipedia summary of 2011 Wisconsin Act 10; PBS Wisconsin; Wisconsin Public Radio; Wisconsin Examiner reporting on the 2024 legal challenge
confidence: moderate -- well-documented pattern via multiple reputable secondary/journalistic sources but primary text not pulled and the law is under active legal challenge
notes: A December 2024 Dane County ruling struck down the safety/general-employee distinction as an equal-protection violation under the Wisconsin Constitution; status as of this session is unsettled likely under appeal
project_implication: Directly relevant to public-safety institutional privilege (H7/H8) -- shows the safety/non-safety distinction is not treated as self-evidently constitutional everywhere it appears

employee_groups_covered: Security employees, peace officers, firefighters, and fire-department paramedics
police_fire_specific: yes plus an explicit security-employees category broader than just police/fire
impasse_process_type: Automatic mediation 30 days before contract expiration; binding arbitration by either party's request if mediation fails within 15 days
primary_source_used: 5 ILCS 315/14 (Illinois Public Labor Relations Act); Illinois Labor Relations Board decision documents; Illinois General Assembly official statute text
notes: Broader defined covered-group and more procedurally automatic (fixed mediation-timing trigger) than most other states audited
project_implication: Useful contrast case showing scope and procedural triggers vary even among police/fire-specific-arbitration states
